sections.title: Citizenship
sections.subtitle:
Plutarch
Current Events
Canadian Gov./ Economics
Community Read Alouds
Ethics
sections.subject: Citizenship

entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
This course includes the following topic(s): Plutarch: Grades 6-8, Canadian Gov./ Economics: Grade 8, Ethics: Part 2, Current Events: Grades 5-8, Community Read Alouds: Grades 1-12

About Plutarch: Grades 6-8
Plutarch’s Lives is a collection of biographies written 2,000 years ago, which has influenced prominent thinkers from Montaigne to Bacon to Shakespeare. Plutarch’s stories are often difficult to read (and are therefore often dropped by teachers), but their value to help students evaluate the characteristics that define a good citizen cannot be overstated. The lesson plans will contain additional support to what is already included in the Anne White guides.

About Current Events: Grades 5-8
Students are exposed to current events by reading from a variety of sources and having age-appropriate conversations with teachers. Topics such as scientific discoveries, the arts, and human interest stories are generally suitable.

About Canadian Gov./ Economics: Grade 8
Students will spend the first two terms on the history and structure of Canada's constitutional monarchy and on the Charter of Rights and Freedoms. They will then end the year with an introduction to economics, focusing on the history of money, the banking system, and personal finance.

About Community Read Alouds: Grades 1-12
This is meant to be a read-aloud for all ages to enjoy together in the afternoon at least 1 time a week. We encourage you to choose a book that is specific to your region. However, we will offer some suggestions in the Grades 1-3 Literature lessons and Grades 4+ Citizenship lessons. The suggested book titles will be available June 1st in the lesson plans.

About Ethics: Part 2
Students will explore characteristics shared by all Image-Bearers and learn how to guard against good servants becoming bad masters by continuing Charlotte Mason's text written directly to students.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content:
Plutarch: Grades 6-8
Students in 5th grade or new to Plutarch may want to take the Beginner's Track, which covers the life of Publicola over two terms. It is advisable to have students read Beesly’s Stories From the History of Rome and complete one year of Shakespeare before beginning Plutarch.

Canadian Gov./ Economics: Grade 8
The book Canada's Constitutional Monarchy will be read over two years (Grades 8 and 9). However, since this is the first year this book will be introduced, Grades 8 and 9 will have the same readings in 2026-2027.
If a student hasn't studied Canada's government yet, consider having them take the Grade 7 government course first. 

Ethics: Part 2
Ourselves by Charlotte Mason is scheduled over five years. In a multi-age context, students can jump into the book wherever the class is reading. Otherwise, students should begin the book in Part 1.

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

Citizenship: Grade 8 (Canada)
☐ Make sure your student sets up a Citizenship Notebook. They will need a 3-ring binder, lined notebook paper, and 5 Tabbed Dividers. You can read more about how to set one up and suggestions for various sections in the document in Quick Links. 

☐ As desired, plan for a read aloud time. Suggestions are provided in the Community Read Aloud Quick Link, but local favorites are highly recommended whenever possible.

☐ Make a Calendar of Events for Current Events lessons by drawing a calendar grid in a notebook (or print a blank calendar from the internet).

Plutarch: Grades 6-8
☐ Watch the video in Quick Links about teaching Plutarch.

entries.title: Term Prep & Teacher Tips
entries.content:
Canadian Gov./ Economics: Grade 8
In Term 2 students will be asked to do online research about their own local municipal, provincial, and federal elected officials. These assignments should be done with parental supervision to ensure internet safety.

books.title: Historical Atlas of the World 6th Ed.
Dollars and Sense: A Kid's Guide to Using - Not Losing - Money

links.label: Video: How to Teach Plutarch (see Citizenship: Section 7)
The World and Everything In It

blocks.text:
Plutarch: Teachers will find it helpful to preread the entire Life first, and then preread the day’s passage before each lesson. Mark any passages that should be omitted due to content. (In Anne White’s Plutarch Project books, the recommended source for this course, objectionable content has already been omitted.)

Current Events: Preview the news source for suitability.

Most other citizenship topics don't require additional prep.
Connect back to the previous lesson. (Optional for Current Events).

For news sources, show students how to scan the headlines to get an overview first and then choose a story to read. 

For Plutarch or other books: Use a picture, a map, or an engaging question to prepare students to receive the story as relevant. You may talk about one or two unfamiliar words that will be crucial to understanding. It is not necessary to cover all unfamiliar words.

Read the selection assigned or the news story chosen. For rich, dense, or more challenging books such as Ourselves and Plutarch, it is a good idea to read them aloud. Have students keep an eye out for things to add to their Citizenship Notebook, Book of Centuries, and/or Commonplace book.
Have the students narrate orally or in writing.

Give students an opportunity to share their thoughts on the story and any connections made. For current events, look up where events occurred on a map and ask questions to help students discern the quality of the piece. (e.g., Are there sources cited? Is there an obvious bias? If so, what do other authors have to say on this subject? Are arguments supported by evidence? Is that evidence credible?) Consider reading an alternative perspective.
Have students record important ideas in their Citizenship Notebooks.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 2 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book 1

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.VIII p.73-75 "The Desire of Society" - "get at it."

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Choose a habit you would like to cultivate and tell a little about why. Draw a chart for keeping up with your new habit and check in daily.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• COMPOSITION - Journal
Tell of a time when your desire for society was met. How did that experience impact/affect you?

lessons.title: 15m Current Events: Grades 5-8 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Tracker
⬚  ⬚  ⬚  ⬚  ⬚  

News source of your choice
PREP: Preview news source

➜ INTRO
Let's scan the headlines and see what is in the news. What sounds interesting to you? Let's make sure we read a variety of stories. (sports, arts and leisure, politics, op-eds, world news, local news).

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
News story

• What are your thoughts? What sources do they cite? Is there an obvious bias? Are arguments supported by evidence? Is that evidence credible?

➜ LOCATE
Look at a map and find where the events occurred

➜ NOTEBOOK
Write the date and summary of the event in the Current Events section of your Citizenship Notebook.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 2 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book 1

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Ch.VIII p.75-77 "Dangers attending" - "the great world."

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log. Start a chart where you can list desirable human character traits on one side and their daemons on the other.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• COMPOSITION
Tell of a time when you took a "share in the interests of the great world" by listening with all your heart to someone who shared about a profession, skill, subject, or experience with which you were unfamiliar.

lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book 1

➜ NOTE
Be sure to narrate in a variety of ways.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.VIII p.112-115  
"We all have Courage." - "the unbelieving."

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update character traits chart. Think of a new habit you would like to cultivate and create a tracking chart.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• COMPOSITION
Tell how we are able to "take the present without any fearful looking forward." Provide three examples.

lessons.title: 25m Canadian Gov./ Economics: Grade 8 - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Dollars & Sense

➜ INTRO
Are you familiar with the Bible verse, "The love of money is a root of all kinds of evil" (I Timothy 6:10)?  What do you think that means, and how have you seen that truth play out? This term, we will be studying economics which has to do with money and the way a country uses and produces goods and services.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Introduction ix-xiii "Bread, Dough." - "dollars and cents."
Also, go to Ch.1 and read the section at the bottom of p.8 "You've Got It and I Want It: The Law of Supply and Demand"

• How does money help the economy, and how does greed hurt the economy?

• Explain the economic principle "The Law of Supply and Demand."
∞ Video Link: What is Supply and Demand?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Make a list of the goods and services your family uses that contribute toward a healthy economy. Goods are tangible things you buy, such as clothes and food. Services are intangible and usually involve paying for a business to do something for you. Examples: haircuts, car insurance, phone service.

Can you think of examples that fit this economic principle? Also, make a list of items that may be in high demand or low demand depending on the time of year.

Ch.1 Skulls, Sheep, and Shells,  p.1-9 "Have you ever" - "the ancient world."

• Why did the bartering system need to be replaced with something better?

• Talk about the different items used before money and why they didn't work out.

∞ Image Link: Cowrie shells used as money in ancient China
∞ Image Link: Deerskin used as folding money (China)
∞ Image Link: Chinese paper money
∞ Image Link: Fort Knox
lessons.teacherNotes:

lessons.title: 25m Canadian Gov./ Economics: Grade 8 - Lesson 27
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Dollars & Sense, Historical Atlas of the World

➜ RECAP
What do you remember about last week's reading?

➜ LOCATE
Using the Historical Atlas of the World p.8, find Mesopotamia and Babylon. What present-day countries are located there?
Go to p.30-31, find Jerusalem, Edessa, Antioch and Tripoli. Now, find Florence, Italy. These are places that we will read about today that have to do with an early form of banking. 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.2 Worry, Worry, Worry! p.11-19 (Also read sections on p.12 "An Eye for an Eye" and p.14 "You Can't Take it With You."
"Where do you keep" - "Hard work."

• What banking practices from history are still used today?

• How did the Knights of Templar help the Pilgrims while traveling to Jerusalem?
∞ Image Link: The Knights Templar
lessons.teacherNotes:
• IMPORTANT DATES

lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Dollars & Sense

➜ RECAP
What do you remember about last week's reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.3 New Money for a New Nation p.21- 29 
"In early America" - "finally in place." (Also, go back and read the section about Alexander Hamilton on p.22.)

• What were some reasons some people were opposed to a national bank?

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Look at the map on p.27 which shows the location of Federal Reserve Banks. What bank is your city part of? You can check out this link to see the 12 districts.
∞ Image Link: The Twelve Federal Reserve Districts
lessons.teacherNotes:
• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK

∞ Link: Federal Reserve Board
∞ Link: Comptroller of the Currency

• EXTRA HELPING
Watch this 3-minute video on this website that explains the purpose of the Federal Reserve System.
∞ Video Link: Federal Reserve System

• IMPORTANT DATES
Establishment of the first national bank of the United States (1791)

The Federal Reserve Banks were established (1913)

lessons.title: 25m Canadian Gov./ Economics: Grade 8 - Lesson 31
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Dollars & Sense

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time in economics?

➜ INTRO
What do you think it would be like to become the President in the middle of the Great Depression? 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.5 Money Goes Away p.49-53 "Franklin Delano Roosevelt" - "the Great Recession." 

➜ NOTE
You don't need to read the excerpt "Thinking about Money" on p.52. It will be read in the next lesson. 

• What are some things that President Roosevelt did to help the economy? Why did some people not agree with those actions?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Make a list of some of the economic programs that were part of the New Deal. You can find the information on p.53 and at the following website.
 ∞ Link: New Deal Programs

• EXTRA HELPING
You can look to see what public works were created in your state as a result of the New Deal which employed thousands of people. 
∞ Link: New Deal projects by state

• IMPORTANT DATES
New Deal programs are established by FDR (1933)

➜ INTRO
Do you know what a mortgage is? How does it help people buy houses?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.6 Money Shrinks p.55-61 "Most people " - "in the future." 

• Explain what we mean by a housing bubble and how that was one of the factors that led to the Great Recession.

• On p.61 the author writes, "Once again, Keynesian economics came into play as the US government stepped in to institute large and controversial programs."

• Go back to p.52 and read the excerpt about the famous economist John Maynard Keynes. Discuss his theories and how they came into play during the Great Recession.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Make a list of the ways the government stepped in to stimulate the economy during the Great Recession. You can find information in the excerpts on p.60-61.

• IMPORTANT DATES
Great Recession (December, 2007-June, 2009)

⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book 1

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.XI p.131-133 "It is a seemly fashion" - "have given away."

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• COMPOSITION - Script
Write a few scenes in which you show through the characters that gladness is catching.

lessons.title: 25m Canadian Gov./ Economics: Grade 8 - Lesson 34
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Dollars & Sense

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time in Economics?

➜ INTRO
Why do you think having a budget is a wise financial practice?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.8 Where Did My Money Go? p.73-76 "Money is - "paying the bills." 

• What is the difference between needs and wants and how does that affect a budget?

• Many people suggest three categories for budgeting—spending, saving, and giving. What do you think about those 3 categories and how can you go about implementing that?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Practice putting together a budget. Use the example on p.74 and create a chart in your notebook. If you don't have your own money or expenses, you can try to put one together to show the expenses of an average household and see what kind of income you would need to cover those expenses.

lessons.title: 15m Current Events: Grades 5-8 - Lesson 34
lessons.body:
Tracker
⬚  ⬚  ⬚  ⬚  ⬚  

News source of your choice
PREP: Preview news source

➜ INTRO
Let's scan the headlines and see what is in the news. What sounds interesting to you? Let's make sure we read a variety of stories. (sports, arts and leisure, politics, op-eds, world news, local news).

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
News story

• What are your thoughts? What sources do they cite? Is there an obvious bias? Are arguments supported by evidence? Is that evidence credible?

➜ LOCATE
Look at a map and find where the events occurred

➜ NOTEBOOK
Write the date and summary of the event in the Current Events section of your Citizenship Notebook.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 2 - Lesson 35
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book 1

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.XI p.133-135 "Gladness is Perennial." - "I say, rejoice."

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Write a brief reflection on how you did with habit cultivation this term.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS
Act out a scene or two in which someone is at a delightful event, but he or she is sullen and unhappy. What was the cause of the person's moodiness? How did it affect his or her time at the event among the other guests? How did it affect other guests?

lessons.title: 25m Canadian Gov./ Economics: Grade 8 - Lesson 35
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Dollars & Sense

➜ RECAP
What did you read last time in Economics?

➜ INTRO
Do you know how much debt the U.S. has? Take a guess. You can find the answer on this website:
∞ Link: National Debt

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.8 Where Did My Money Go? p.77-86, Also read excerpts on p.78, 82.
Conclusion, p.85-86 "It's not just" - "no matter what." 

• Why do you think it is so hard to balance the national budget?
lessons.teacherNotes:
• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Make a list of some of the items that the federal government spends money on. Make a list of ways the government receives money (revenue). Try to brainstorm by yourself and then you can use these websites to find more information.
∞ Link: Government Spending
∞ Link: Sources of Federal Revenue
